questions.description: Answer parts (a), (b), and (c) (a) Assess, with reference to relevant EU Treaty provisions and case-law, whether there…
questions.text: Answer parts (a), (b), and (c) (a) Assess, with reference to relevant EU Treaty provisions and case-law, whether there is a discernible separation of legislative and executive powers in the EU system. Your answer should also consider: (b) How such separation as exists is affected by the role of the European Council under Article 15 TEU, as inserted by the Treaty of Lisbon. (c) How such separation as exists is affected by the formalisation and expansion of the role of national parliaments in the EU legislative process in Article 12 TEU and Protocol Nos. 1 and 2 to the Treaty of Lisbon.

Answer part (A) [or]{.underline} (B). Do [not]{.underline} attempt both parts. A) Discuss, with reference to relevant Treaty provisions, particularly any relevant new provisions introduced by the Treaty of Lisbon, and relevant case-law, the development of: (i) the scope of application; and (ii) the sources of fundamental rights protection in EU law. B) Article 15 TFEU (formerly Article 255(1) EC), which was originally inserted by the Treaty of Amsterdam with effect from 1^st^ May 1999, provides that: "[A]ny citizen of the Union, and any natural or legal person residing or having its registered office in a Member State, shall have a right of access to European Parliament, Council and Commission documents, subject to the principles and the conditions to be defined in accordance with this paragraph". Analyse the extent to which the case-law of the Union judicature guarantees for EU citizens an effective right of public access to documents held by EU institutions?

questions.description: Answer parts (a) and (b) (a) Analyse by reference to relevant case-law the interpretation by the Union judicature of…
questions.text: Answer parts (a) and (b) (a) Analyse by reference to relevant case-law the interpretation by the Union judicature of the standing requirements of "direct and individual concern" under the fourth paragraph of what is now Article 263 TFEU (formerly Article 230 EC) for natural or legal persons. Are the requirements interpreted too strictly? (b) Consider the significance of the amendment to Article 263 TFEU introduced by the Treaty of Lisbon pertaining to "regulatory" acts that "do not entail implementing measures."

questions.description: Write a note on [any three]{.underline} of the following cases, setting out their core facts, the key issues of EU law…
questions.text: Write a note on [any three]{.underline} of the following cases, setting out their core facts, the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration and how those issues were addressed by EU courts: (i) Case 26/62 Van Gend en Loos [1963] ECR 1; (ii) Case 152/84 Marshall v Southampton Area Health Authority [1986] ECR 723; (iii) Case C-184/99 Grzelczyk [2001] ECR I-6193; (iv) Case T-342/99 Airtours v Commission [2002] ECR II-2585; (v) Case C-280/00 Altmark Trans and Regierungsprasidium Magdeburg [2003] ECR I-7747; (vi) Case C-224/01 Kobler [2003] ECR I-10239; Joined Cases C-397/01 to C-403/01 Pfeiffer and others [2004] ECR I-8835

questions.description: Consider both of the following situations: (i) COMAIR, the (hypothetical)… Damages, Directive.
questions.text: Consider both of the following situations: (i) COMAIR, the (hypothetical) Irish aviation regulator, calculates landing fees at Dublin airport for the period 2010 to 2013 in a manner that violates a (fictitious) EU aviation Directive. The Directive has not been transposed into Irish law, although the implementation date has passed. COMAIR grants a private company, FE-1 Air, landing slots at Dublin airport subject to acceptance of the notified landing fees. FE-1 Air fails to challenge COMAIR's decision determining the landing fees within the one-month limitation period provided under the relevant Irish rules. Advise FE-1 Air as to whether EU law entitles it to refuse to pay the landing fees and whether it may challenge the legality of the fees under the Directive even though the one-month limitation period in Irish law has passed. (ii) FE-2 Car Parks, a private company engaged in the provision of off-street car parking services in Ireland, brought an unsuccessful High Court challenge based on a (fictitious) EU VAT Directive. The High Court rejected FE-2's argument that the VAT exemption enjoyed by local authorities in respect of the off-street parking they provide was incompatible with the Directive. FE-2 appealed to the Supreme Court. In the meantime, the Court of Justice of the European Union, in a case the Commission brought under Article 258 TFEU (formerly Article 226 EC) against Ireland, held that the exemption was indeed incompatible with that Directive. Advise FE-2 Car Parks regarding the import of the Court of Justice's judgment for: (i) its appeal before the Supreme Court; and (ii) its possible entitlement to damages under EU law arising out of Ireland's mis-implementation of the Directive and the High Court's error of EU law.

questions.description: Assess the compatibility of each of the following (hypothetical) Irish measures and practices with EU law and in…
questions.text: Assess the compatibility of each of the following (hypothetical) Irish measures and practices with EU law and in particular with Articles 28 and 30 TFEU, Articles 34 and 36 TFEU, Article 49 TFEU, and Article 110 TFEU (formerly Articles 23 and 25 EC, Articles 28 and 30 EC, Article 43 EC and Article 90 EC): (i) Waste regulations adopted by all of Dublin's four local authorities imposing a charge per kilogramme on business and residential waste collected in the Dublin area and applied when it is moved for disposal and/or recycling outside of Dublin. The proceeds of the charge are used by the local authorities for general budgetary purposes. Licensed waste operators which collect in the Dublin area and which do not transport it outside for disposal and/or recycling pay no corresponding charge. The authorities' aim is to encourage operators to dispose of their waste at a new incineration plant near the city, which is partially owned by the four local authorities; (ii) Under a 'Confectionery Hygiene' regulation the Minister for Health and Children has prohibited the marketing of sugar confectionery, or similar products made using sugar substitutes, without wrapping from vending machines. Such products are lawfully marketed from vending machines in the United Kingdom (including Northern Ireland) and in most other Member States; (iii) A new provision in the Pharmacy Acts which prohibits the owning, operation or control of pharmacies by persons not having the status of pharmacist and limiting the number of pharmacies that any individual pharmacist may own, operate or control to a maximum of three, all of which must be located within a 50-kilometre radius of each other. The Government claims that these measures are necessary to protect consumers of medicaments by rendering individual pharmacists fully responsible for products sold in chemists and reducing the likelihood that the maximisation of profit margins might overly influence the advice given therein to consumers.

Answer parts (i), (ii) [and]{.underline} (iii). Note: These scenarios arise after the implementation date of Directive 2004/38/EC on the rights of citizens of the union and their family members to move and reside freely within the territory of the Member States. Candidates should have particular regard to the potential relevance of citizenship case-law. (i) Patrick, an Irish national, was arrested whilst on holiday at a night club in Spain and charged with possession of an offensive weapon. He understands that under Spanish criminal procedure trials are normally conducted in Spanish, but that an accused person has the right to opt to have his/her trial conducted in either the Catalan or Basque languages. Spanish nationals, or EU nationals resident in Spain for at least five years, may, in the further alternative, opt for a trial in English. Patrick, who is resident in London, would like his trial procedure conducted in English and seeks your advice as to whether, having regard to the Spanish criminal procedure linguistic regime, he would be entitled, under EU law, to opt for a trial in English; (ii) Michael, an Irish national, went to Belgium in 2007, where he has now resided for almost three years, first at a campsite near a seaside resort and then from December 2008 in Brussels initially at a youth hostel and then in a Salvation Army hostel, where, in return for board and lodging and some pocket money, he does various jobs for about 30 hours a week as part of a personal social-occupational reintegration programme. He has no savings but applied for a state minimum subsistence allowance so as to be able to leave the hostel and live independently. The Belgian social assistance authorities have refused his application as he is not a Belgian national and not a worker or self-employed. Michael seeks your advice as to whether the refusal is compatible with EU law; (iii) Alice is Irish, and wishes to establish a clinic providing a counselling service for pregnant women in Malta, where Alice has been working as a psychologist since 2007. In Malta, the intentional medical termination of a pregnancy is strictly prohibited, as is the dissemination of any advertisement for, or information or advice about the availability of, such medical terminations in other countries, however supplied. Alice seeks your advice as to whether she may rely on EU law to allow abortion clinics based in other Member States, where such services are legal, to advertise at her clinic, and whether she may provide, in return for a fee, information and advice regarding abortion options in other Member States.

questions.description: Answer part (A) [or]{.underline} (B). Advise the Irish government on whether there are grounds to challenge the…
questions.text: Answer part (A) [or]{.underline} (B). Do [not]{.underline} attempt both parts (A) "The essence of Article 82 (EC) [now Article 102 TFEU] is the control of market power, whether by a single firm or, subject to certain conditions, a number of firms. Article 82 does not prohibit market power per se" (Craig and De Burca, EU Law, 4^th^ ed., at p. 1005). Assess the interpretation of Article 82 [EC] [now Article 102 TFEU] in the relevant case-law of the EU courts in light of this statement. (B) Ireland exempted from exercise duty fossil fuels used for production of alumina in the Shannon region between 1983 and 2005. In 2005, the exemption was replaced with a reduced rate. The Commission, which had expressed concerns to the Government regarding the exemption, initiated in 2006 the procedure provided for in Article 108(2) TFEU (formerly Article 88(2) EC) with regard both to the exemption and reduced rate. In its resulting decision, the Commission has decided that both the exemption and resulting decision, the Commission has decided that both the exemption and reduced rate are new State aids that are incompatible with the common market. However, its decision only orders Ireland to recover the difference between the reduced rate and new State aids that are incompatible with the common market. However, its decision and the normal rate of exercise duty from the date of introduction of the reduced rate in 2005. Advise the Irish government on whether there are grounds to challenge the Commission decision before the EU courts. Explain the procedure to be followed for such a challenge.

questions.description: "Fundamental rights, as guaranteed by the European Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental…
questions.text: "Fundamental rights, as guaranteed by the European Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms and as they result from constitutional traditions common to the Member States, shall constitute general principles of the Union's law" (Article 6(3) TEU, as amended by the Treaty of Lisbon). (i) Discuss the development of the scope of application and the sources of fundamental rights protection in EU law. (ii) Is that development likely to be affected by Article 6(1) of the TEU, as amended, by the Treaty of Lisbon, which provides for recognition by the Union of the rights and freedoms set out in the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union and for those rights and freedoms to have "the same legal value as the Treaties"?

Answer part (a) [or]{.underline} (b). Do [not]{.underline} attempt both parts. (a) "The limits of Union competence are governed by the principle of conferral. The use of Union competences is governed by the principles of subsidiarity and proportionality" (Article 5 TEU (formerly Article 5EC)). Assess, by reference in particular to relevant case-law and legal literature, whether the principles of subsidiarity and proportionality have, since their formal introduction by the Treaty of Maastricht in 1993, acted as an effective constraint on the adoption of legislation by the Union (formerly the European Community). Evaluate the likely impact of the changes introduced by the Treaty of Lisbon, in particular by the 'Protocol on the Application of the Principles of Subsidiarity and Proportionality' in this regard. (b) Article 15 TFEU (formerly Article 255(1) EC), which was originally inserted by the Treaty of Amsterdam with effect from 1^st^ May 1999, provides that: "[A]ny citizen of the Union, and any natural or legal person residing or having its registered office in a Member State, shall have a right of access to European Parliament, Council and Commission documents, subject to the principles and the conditions to be defined in accordance with this paragraph". Analyse the extent to which the case-law of the Union judicature guarantees for EU citizens an effective right of public access to documents held by EU institutions?

questions.description: Consider whether the Brussels Convention of 27^th^ September 1968 on Jurisdiction and the Enforcement of Judgements in…
questions.text: Consider whether the Brussels Convention of 27^th^ September 1968 on Jurisdiction and the Enforcement of Judgements in Civil and Commercial Matters, as now largely replaced by Council Regulation (EC) No. 44/2001 on Jurisdiction and the Enforcement of Judgements in Civil and Commercial Matters ("the Brussels I Regulation"), facilitates the progressive establishment of 'an area of freedom, justice and security' as envisaged by Article 67 TFEU (formerly Article 61 EC) by promoting clear jurisdictional rules for determining which courts have jurisdiction in civil and commercial matters involving inter-state elements. In your answer refer, in particular, to the scope and operation of the principal jurisdictional rule under Article 2(1) of Regulation No. 44/2001 and the special rules laid down by Article 5(1) and (3).

questions.description: Write a note on [any three]{.underline} of the following seven cases, setting out their core facts, the key issues of…
questions.text: Write a note on [any three]{.underline} of the following seven cases, setting out their core facts, the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration and how those issues were addressed by the EU courts: (i) Case 22/70 Commission v ("ERTA") [1971] ECR 263; (ii) Case C-188/89 Foster v British Gas [1990] ECR I-3133; (iii) Case C-280/00 Altmark Trans and Regierungspräsidium Magdeburg [2003] ECR I- 7747; (iv) Case C-200/02 Zhu and Chen [2004] ECR I-9925; (v) Case T-201/04 Microsoft v Commission [2007] ECR II-3601; (vi) Case C-341/05 Laval un Partneri [2007] ECR I-11767; (vii) Joined Cases C-402/06 P and C-415/05 P Kadi and Al Barakaat International Foundation v Council [2008] ECR I-6351.

questions.description: Answer both parts (i) and (ii): (i) FE-1 Transport is a haulage company… Damages, Directive.
questions.text: Answer both parts (i) and (ii): (i) FE-1 Transport is a haulage company that was precluded for many years under Irish VAT legislation from claiming VAT deductions in its VAT returns in respect of inputs (costs) relating to the transport of certain goods. The Commission later brought infringement proceedings against Ireland, wherein the Court of Justice determined that the relevant Irish VAT provisions were incompatible with the EU VAT Directive. The Oireachtas repealed the Irish legislation six months after the Court's judgment. FE-1 Transport has sought reimbursement of both the total of the deductions it was not allowed make during the six months between the Court's judgment and the repeal of the offending Irish provisions and the total of the deductions it was not allowed during the three years immediately preceding the Court's judgment. The Irish tax authorities are not prepared to reimburse any of the deductions. They claim that a Member State is allowed reasonable time to comply with Court judgments and that a period of six months is reasonable. As regards the period prior to the Court's judgment, whilst admitting the general limitation period for claims against them under the VAT legislation is three years, the tax authorities claim that no reimbursement is payable to FE-1 Transport because the relevant provision of the VAT Directive was not directly effective and, if it was, FE-1 could have but did not either bring legal proceedings to challenge the provisions precluding the deductions or contest, in any of its two-monthly VAT returns during the three-year period in question, the non-availability of the deductions. Advise FE-1 Transport as to whether the VAT Directive could be relied upon directly by it and, if so, whether EU law would entitle it to reimbursement of the precluded deductions. (ii) FE-2 Ferries was a private company engaged in the provision of ferry services to several islands off the west coast of Ireland. It was in competition with Western Boats Limited for several years but went out of business due to its inability to compete with the low fares offered by Western Boats, and particularly Western Boat's policy of only charging island residents token fares for travel to the mainland and back. The liquidator of FE-2 discovered that Western Boats was in receipt of substantial subsidies from the Western Development Agency throughout the period during which FE-2 Ferries was in competition with it. The liquidator brought an action for damages for breach of the EU competition and State aid rules in the High Court. This was dismissed by the High Court, which held that there was no effect on trade between Member States and so the competition rules in Articles 101, 102 and 106 TFEU (formerly Articles 81, 82 and 86 EC) were inapplicable the High Court also considered that the subsidies were justified to facilitate travel to and from the islands by island residents, and that, accordingly, no breach of the State aid rules of Articles 107 and 108 TFEU (formerly Articles 87 and 88 EC) had occurred. This decision was upheld on appeal by the Supreme Court, which refused a request from the liquidator for a reference to the Court of Justice. Following the Supreme Court decision, the Commission found, following an investigation, that the subsidies constituted unlawful State aid in that they had never been notified to it and, furthermore, were incompatible with the common market. The liquidator is now considering bringing an action for damages in the High Court against Ireland for the breach of EU law regarding both the grant of the subsidies to Western Boats and the Supreme Court decision. Advise FE-2 Ferries' liquidator regarding the relevant EU law principles concerning damages for breach of EU law as they emerge from the case-law of the Court of Justice and, based thereon, as to the liquidator's chances of success.

questions.description: Assess the compatibility of each of the following (hypothetical) Irish measures and practices with EU law and in…
questions.text: Assess the compatibility of each of the following (hypothetical) Irish measures and practices with EU law and in particular with Articles 28 and 30 TFEU, Articles 34 and 35 TFEU, Article 49 TFEU, Article 56, and Article 110 TFEU (formerly Articles 23 and 25 EC, Articles 28 and 30 EC, Article 43 EC, Article 49 EC and Article 90 EC): (i) A 'carbon levy' of 0.2 cent per unit of electricity supplied for consumption, save in respect of units of electricity generated from renewable energy sources within the State and supplied to the national grid in respect of which a 'green energy promotion levy' of 0.1 cent is applied. The percentage of units subject to the reduced levy on each individual customer's electricity bill is calculated solely by reference to the percentage of the total units supplied for consumption in the State in the previous year that originated from renewable energy generating sources within the State. The proceeds from the 'green energy promotion levy' collected from consumers by the suppliers of electricity are remitted to the Department of Communications, Energy and Natural Resources, which uses them to fun grants for new wind farm projects within the State; (ii) A new provision in the Pharmacy Acts which prohibits the owning, operation or control of pharmacies by persons not having the status of pharmacist and limiting the number of pharmacies that any individual pharmacist may own, operate or control to a maximum of three, all of which must be located within a 50-kilometre radius of each other. The Government claims that these measures are necessary to protect consumers of medicaments by rendering individual pharmacists fully responsible for products sold in chemists and reducing the likelihood that the maximisation of profit margins might overly influence the advice given therein to consumers; (iii) A law that only permits building firms registered on a trade register, which (under the law) is maintained by the Irish Chamber of Commerce on behalf of the Department of Enterprise, Trade and Employment, to be engaged for the provision of building works in the State.

Answer parts (i), (ii) [and]{.underline} (iii). Note: These scenarios arise after the implementation date of Directive 2004/38/EC on the rights of citizens of the union and their family members to move and reside freely within the territory of the Member States. Candidates should have particular regard to the potential relevance of citizenship case-law. (i) Heinrich is an Austrian national who has recently moved to Ireland in search of employment. He applies for a job as a senior IT manager in the Department of Foreign Affairs, but is refused an interview on the basis that the post is reserved for Irish nationals. Ireland argues that EU law permits it to reserve some public sector posts to nationals. Advise Heinrich on whether this is so, and whether the exemption may be applied to the post in question; (ii) Patrick, and Irish national, went to Belgium in 2007, where he has now resided for almost three years, first at a campsite near a seaside resort and then from December 2008 in Brussels initially at a youth hostel and then in a Salvation Army hostel, where, in return for board and lodging and some pocket money, he does various jobs for about 30 hours a week as part of a personal socio-occupational reintegration programme. He has no savings but applied for a state minimum subsistence allowance so as to be able to leave the hostel and live independently. The Belgian social assistance authorities have refused his application as he is not a Belgian national and not a worker or self-employed. Patrick seeks your advice as to whether the refusal is compatible with EU law; (iii) Gheorghe is a Romanian national who travelled to France in search of work. Several months later, he was deported from France on the basis that he was homeless and had no obvious means of supporting himself. The deportation took place within a few days of Gheorghe's arrest and, although he speaks only little French, he was given no opportunity to make submissions against his deportation or challenge it before a French court. Subsequently, back in Romania, he received a letter explaining that the deportation was because of his 'illegal residence' in France. He has also learned that, under pressure from the French authorities, the Romanian Minister for the Interior proposes to apply a Romanian court for an order prohibiting Gheorghe from returning to France for a period of three years. Gheorghe seeks your advice as to whether his deportation from France to Romania was compatible with EU law and as to whether, based on EU law, he could oppose the order that may be sought against him in Romania.

Answer part (a) [or]{.underline} (b). Do [not]{.underline} attempt both parts. (a) Compare and contrast the scope, definition of discrimination and availability of justification relating to the prohibitions of discrimination on the grounds of sex, age and 'racial or ethnic origin' in EU law. In so doing, refer both to relevant Treaty provisions, Directives and caselaw of the Union judicature. (b) Assess critically the interpretation in the case-law of the Union judicature of the following important concepts under Article 101 TFEU (formerly Article 81 EC): (i) "agreements between undertakings"; (ii) "undertakings"; (iii) "concerted practices".
